question: A patient with underlying chronic liver disease underwent an abdominal CT that was performed only in the venous phase. Which diagnostic imaging study is most appropriate next to comprehensively evaluate the liver for possible focal lesions?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the provided image and question. The CT image is labeled as a venous-phase abdominal CT and shows stigmata of chronic liver disease (labelled "cirrhotic liver with no defined mass"), ascites, and an enlarged spleen. The question states the CT was performed only in the venous phase and asks which study is most appropriate next to comprehensively evaluate the liver for possible focal lesions.

Next, I relate these observations to the diagnostic goal. Because the available CT is limited to the venous phase, it does not provide a full multiphasic assessment of liver enhancement over time. A comprehensive evaluation for focal liver lesions therefore requires an imaging study that assesses multiple contrast phases or otherwise provides superior lesion characterization compared with a single-phase venous CT.

Then I consider how each option aligns with that requirement. Option A explicitly offers multiphasic contrast-enhanced liver MRI, which provides multiphase contrast assessment targeted to the liver. Option B (triphasic CT) would also provide arterial, portal, and delayed phases, improving on the current single venous-phase CT. Options C (abdominal ultrasound with Doppler) and D (contrast-enhanced ultrasound, targeted) are more limited in scope compared with whole-liver multiphasic cross-sectional studies.

Finally, synthesizing the image findings (cirrhosis, ascites, splenomegaly) and the need for a comprehensive, multiphase liver assessment, the best next study is a dedicated multiphasic contrast-enhanced liver MRI (option A), because it directly provides the multiphasic, liver-focused evaluation called for by the question.
caption: Standard abdominal computed tomography with contrast showing splenomegaly, ascites, and no defined hepatic mass.
sub_caption: Standard abdominal computed tomography with contrast showing splenomegaly, ascites, and no defined hepatic mass.
image_context: A 61-year-old obese woman presented to the clinic with a complaint of generalized abdominal pain, exertional dyspnea, and worsening lower extremity edema. She had a medical history significant for HCV cirrhosis, treated with a sustained virologic response. She denied any history of cardiac disease, jaundice, or pruritus. She endorsed compliance with recommended daily sodium intake and medications. Examination findings were unremarkable, except for a distended abdomen, positive shifting dullness, and bilateral pitting lower extremity edema. Laboratory findings showed a white blood cell count of 3.33k/cmm (4.5–10), hemoglobin of 12.3 g/dL(12–16), platelet of 109k/cmm (150–440), international normalized ratio of 1.12 (0.9–1.3), Cr of 0.62 mg/dL (0.7–1.3), gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase of 359 unit/L (5–85), alkaline phosphatase of 159 unit/L (15–37), aspartate aminotransferase of 159 unit/L (15–37), alanine aminotransferase of 59 unit/L (12–78), total bilirubin of 1.2 mg/dL (0.2–1.0), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) of >20,000 ng/mL (0.5–8.0), CA 125 of 505 unit/mL (1.5-35), CA 19-9 of 40U/mL (0–35), peritoneal fluid cytology was negative, model for end-stage liver disease of 16, and Child-Pugh B. A right upper quadrant ultrasound showed cirrhosis without evidence of a hepatic mass. A standard abdominal CT scan obtained in the venous phase showed splenomegaly and cirrhosis with no hepatic mass (Figure <xref rid="ac9-9-e00945-g001" ref-type="fig">1</xref>). Further studies with multiphasic MRI with contrast showed cirrhosis with portal vein thrombosis and cavernous transformation; no hypervascular hepatic mass was seen (Figure ). Further studies with multiphasic MRI with contrast showed cirrhosis with portal vein thrombosis and cavernous transformation; no hypervascular hepatic mass was seen (Figure <xref rid="ac9-9-e00945-g002" ref-type="fig">2</xref>). The markedly elevated AFP left a high suspicion for HCC; hence, the patient was referred for EUS-FNA, which showed 12 × 16 mm nonocclusive portal vein thrombosis and heterogeneous and diffuse echogenicity worrisome for an infiltrative process (Figure ). The markedly elevated AFP left a high suspicion for HCC; hence, the patient was referred for EUS-FNA, which showed 12 × 16 mm nonocclusive portal vein thrombosis and heterogeneous and diffuse echogenicity worrisome for an infiltrative process (Figure <xref rid="ac9-9-e00945-g003" ref-type="fig">3</xref>). The biopsy report showed malignant cells positive for AFP while negative for trypsin, arginase, SF1, chromogranin, synaptophysin, and PAX 8. Immunostains marked tumor cells reactive for glypican-3 and negative for Hep Par1, supporting the diagnosis of HCC (Figure ). The biopsy report showed malignant cells positive for AFP while negative for trypsin, arginase, SF1, chromogranin, synaptophysin, and PAX 8. Immunostains marked tumor cells reactive for glypican-3 and negative for Hep Par1, supporting the diagnosis of HCC (Figure <xref rid="ac9-9-e00945-g004" ref-type="fig">4</xref>). On a follow-up visit, an oncology referral was discussed with the patient and she was agreeable. A month later, the patient died before she could follow up with oncology.). On a follow-up visit, an oncology referral was discussed with the patient and she was agreeable. A month later, the patient died before she could follow up with oncology.
relevant_image_context: A standard abdominal CT scan obtained in the venous phase showed splenomegaly and cirrhosis with no hepatic mass (Figure 1).
